text: The patient is positioned with the right side elevated, and three robotic ports are placed along with an assistant port and a port for liver retraction.
The initial laparoscopic view shows a highly vascular tumor located in the right suprarenal region.
The surgeon drops the colon medially and develops a surgical plane between the inferior vena cava and the highly vascular tumor.
The surgeon cuts the hepatic ligaments to mobilize the liver superiorly, and a retractor is placed to maintain the liver in the elevated position.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to control bleeding from the peritumoral vessels, allowing for clearer visualization and further dissection around the tumor.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to gradually expose and manage the tumoral vessels, ensuring hemostasis in the highly vascular area.
The surgeon develops a plane between the upper pole of the kidney and the tumor, revealing additional vascular structures associated with the tumor.

The surgeon clips the vessels using Hem-o-lok clips on both sides and then divides them. Further dissection is performed between the upper pole of the kidney and the suprarenal mass, revealing the anatomical separation between these structures.
The surgeon lifts the tumor from its bed using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, revealing the underlying swath muscle.
The surgeon clips and divides the medial connections of the tumor with the inferior vena cava, ensuring separation from this major vessel.
The surgeon clips and divides all veins draining from the tumor into the inferior vena cava using a harmonic scalpel, ensuring hemostasis.
The surgeon exposes normal adrenal tissue at the superior extent of the tumor, revealing the contrast between the healthy adrenal tissue and the tumor mass.
The surgeon introduces scissors to perform further dissection around the suprarenal mass, compensating for the non-articulating nature of the harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the tumor from its bed, including normal adrenal tissue, by severing the final peritoneal attachments, rendering the tumor free from surrounding structures.
The surgeon places the adrenal tumor specimen into a retrieval bag and extracts it through a small incision.
